case_name: Perchlorate removal from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for perchlorate-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under moderate cost and stable operation constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal treatment plant draws from a river-influenced reservoir and has identified perchlorate as the principal regulated contaminant concern. The utility already operates conventional surface-water clarification and filtration and wants a reliable add-on process that can consistently reduce perchlorate for finished drinking water compliance. The city prefers established technologies with moderate operating burden and does not want to adopt unnecessarily energy-intensive treatment.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable perchlorate reduction suitable for drinking water compliance while maintaining stable overall water quality.
secondary_goals: maintain stable year-round operation for variable surface water
limit energy demand and avoid excessive process complexity
remain compatible with existing conventional treatment infrastructure

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: utility can manage routine backwash waste and periodic media replacement but prefers to avoid difficult brine or high-strength concentrate disposal

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: reduce suspended solids and protect downstream perchlorate removal units from surface-water particulate loading
core_unit_functions.selective_perchlorate_ion_exchange: remove perchlorate using highly selective resin designed for oxyanion control
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish effluent and retain any carryover particles or resin fines
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: perchlorate is the single primary contaminant and selective ion exchange is a well-established drinking water control technology
surface water solids and organics justify use after conventional clarification and filtration rather than direct raw-water polishing alone
the utility already has conventional treatment, making selective ion exchange a practical add-on process
this approach offers stable and predictable contaminant removal without the energy demand of high-pressure membranes
potential_risks: competing anions such as sulfate, nitrate, and natural organic matter can shorten resin run length
resin exhaustion monitoring is required to prevent perchlorate breakthrough
spent resin replacement or regeneration residual handling must be planned carefully

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: remove particulates and reduce upstream fouling load for biological treatment
core_unit_functions.biological_fluidized_bed_or_fixed_bed_pe: biologically reduce perchlorate to chloride under controlled anoxic conditions with an electron donor
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove biomass or detached solids from the biological stage
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure finished water microbiological safety after biological treatment

applicability_rationale: biological perchlorate reduction is a recognized treatment route and can be effective for selective perchlorate removal
it can avoid frequent resin replacement when operated well
the approach is more suitable where the utility can manage process monitoring and biological control
it remains lower energy than reverse osmosis but is operationally more involved than selective ion exchange
potential_risks: requires careful control of electron donor addition and anoxic conditions
biological instability or seasonal temperature effects can affect performance
additional attention is needed to prevent biological solids carryover and maintain disinfectant strategy

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and part of natural organic matter from variable surface water
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: provide clarified water with reduced particle loading to downstream selective resin
core_unit_functions.selective_perchlorate_ion_exchange: remove dissolved perchlorate from clarified water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection in finished water

applicability_rationale: this is a more explicit full-train version of the preferred approach for utilities with conventional surface-water treatment already in place
good pretreatment can improve resin life and operational stability
it is highly compatible with reservoir water having moderate turbidity and DOC
potential_risks: conventional treatment alone will not remove perchlorate, so ion exchange performance remains critical
chemical dose optimization affects pretreatment consistency and resin protection
sludge production from clarification increases solids handling needs

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: control particulate and fouling load before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject perchlorate and other dissolved ions through high-pressure membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after desalting to maintain corrosivity control
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished water

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis can effectively reduce perchlorate
it may be considered when broader dissolved contaminant reduction is also desired
however, for a single perchlorate problem it is usually more complex and costly than needed
potential_risks: high energy demand and membrane fouling sensitivity for surface water
concentrate management is a major disadvantage under moderate constraints

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: reduce solids and foulants before electrically driven desalting
core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_or_electrodialysis_rever: separate perchlorate and other ions using ion-selective membranes under electric potential
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish water quality and remove incidental particulate carryover

applicability_rationale: electrically driven membrane processes can remove ionic contaminants including perchlorate
they may be technically feasible in selected installations
for this straightforward municipal case they are less commonly preferred than selective ion exchange and less aligned with a stable low-complexity strategy
potential_risks: specialized operation and membrane management increase complexity
brine or concentrate disposal remains a disadvantage
surface-water organic and particulate fouling control is important

preferred_rank_pattern: selective_perchlorate_ion_exchange with appropriate surface-water pretreatment should rank first
biological perchlorate reduction may rank high but generally below selective ion exchange when stable low-to-medium complexity operation is preferred
high-energy membrane options should not rank first for a single perchlorate problem under moderate constraints

must_reflect: moderate cost sensitivity favors established targeted treatment over full desalination
stable operation requirement favors robust add-on treatment compatible with existing conventional surface-water systems
residuals and concentrate handling should influence ranking of regeneration-based and membrane options

must_contain: why selective ion exchange is a mainstream treatment for perchlorate in drinking water
why conventional surface-water pretreatment is important before targeted perchlorate removal
why reverse osmosis or other membrane desalting options rank lower for this simple single-contaminant case

should_mention: resin exhaustion and perchlorate breakthrough monitoring
competition from sulfate nitrate and natural organic matter
biological process control needs if bioreduction is selected
concentrate or brine management challenges for membrane-based options
